chief_complaint: Chronic lower back pain with radiculopathy
transcription: PATIENT: 54-year-old male. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Persistent lower back pain radiating to left leg for 8 months. HISTORY: Patient reports progressive worsening of lumbar pain rated 7/10, with radiation down the left leg to the knee following L4-L5 dermatomal pattern. Conservative treatment history: completed 8 weeks of physical therapy (McKenzie protocol) with minimal improvement, NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg BID x 6 weeks) discontinued due to GI intolerance, muscle relaxants (cyclobenzaprine) provided partial relief. IMAGING: MRI lumbar spine dated 2 months ago shows L4-L5 disc herniation with moderate left-sided foraminal stenosis, mild facet arthropathy at L3-L4 and L4-L5. PHYSICAL EXAM: Positive straight leg raise test at 45 degrees left, decreased sensation in L4 distribution, strength 4/5 left dorsiflexion. No bowel or bladder dysfunction. ASSESSMENT: Lumbar radiculopathy secondary to L4-L5 disc herniation. PLAN: Requesting lumbar epidural steroid injection at L4-L5 under fluoroscopic guidance.

chief_complaint: Acute back pain, no prior conservative treatment
transcription: PATIENT: 38-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Low back pain for 3 weeks after lifting injury. HISTORY: Patient reports sudden onset low back pain while lifting furniture rated 6/10. No radiation to extremities. No prior episodes. No imaging performed. No physical therapy or medications tried beyond OTC ibuprofen for 1 week. PHYSICAL EXAM: Lumbar paraspinal muscle tenderness, range of motion limited by pain, negative straight leg raise bilaterally. Neurological exam intact. ASSESSMENT: Acute mechanical low back pain. PLAN: Patient requesting epidural steroid injection for pain relief.

PATIENT: 47-year-old male, BMI 34. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Excessive daytime sleepiness, loud snoring, witnessed apneas for 2 years. HISTORY: Wife reports patient stops breathing 10-15 times per night. Patient falls asleep during meetings, Epworth Sleepiness Scale score 16/24. No prior sleep evaluation. Hypertension on lisinopril. Non-smoker. Neck circumference 42cm. PHYSICAL EXAM: Oropharyngeal crowding, Mallampati Class III, no tonsillar hypertrophy. ASSESSMENT: High clinical suspicion for obstructive sleep apnea based on STOP-BANG score 5/8. PLAN: Requesting attended polysomnography for formal diagnosis and CPAP titration.

PATIENT: 29-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Partner reports occasional snoring. HISTORY: No daytime sleepiness, Epworth score 6/24. No witnessed apneas. No hypertension or cardiovascular disease. BMI 22. Normal neck circumference. No morning headaches. STOP-BANG score 1/8. PHYSICAL EXAM: Normal oropharynx, Mallampati Class I. ASSESSMENT: Possible primary snoring. PLAN: Requesting polysomnography.

chief_complaint: Exertional chest pain with known CAD risk factors
transcription: PATIENT: 62-year-old male. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Exertional chest tightness for 6 weeks. HISTORY: Chest tightness occurring with moderate exertion (climbing 2 flights of stairs), relieved by rest within 5 minutes. Risk factors: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, 30 pack-year smoking history (quit 5 years ago), family history of MI (father at age 58). No prior cardiac workup. ECG shows normal sinus rhythm, no ST changes at rest. PHYSICAL EXAM: BP 148/88, HR 76. No murmurs. Peripheral pulses intact. ASSESSMENT: Stable angina pattern, intermediate pre-test probability for CAD. PLAN: Requesting exercise stress test with imaging for ischemia evaluation.

PATIENT: 35-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Routine annual physical, no symptoms. HISTORY: No chest pain, no dyspnea, no palpitations. No cardiac risk factors. Non-smoker, no hypertension, no diabetes, no family history of early CAD. Exercises regularly (runs 5k three times weekly without symptoms). ECG normal. PHYSICAL EXAM: BP 112/70, HR 64. Normal cardiac exam. ASSESSMENT: Healthy female requesting cardiac stress test for reassurance.

chief_complaint: Medial knee pain with mechanical locking symptoms
transcription: PATIENT: 44-year-old male, recreational soccer player. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Right knee medial pain with intermittent locking for 4 months. HISTORY: Injury occurred during soccer game with twisting mechanism. Medial knee pain rated 6/10, with mechanical locking episodes 3-4 times weekly requiring manual manipulation to unlock. Giving way episodes reported. Completed 6 weeks PT without improvement. NSAIDs provided partial relief. MRI right knee: medial meniscus complex tear with displaced fragment, intact ACL and PCL, no significant chondral damage. PHYSICAL EXAM: McMurray test positive medially, joint line tenderness, mild effusion. ASSESSMENT: Medial meniscus complex tear with mechanical symptoms refractory to conservative management. PLAN: Requesting knee arthroscopy with meniscal repair vs. partial meniscectomy.

PATIENT: 68-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Bilateral knee pain for 5 years. HISTORY: Gradual onset bilateral knee pain, worse with prolonged walking. No locking, no giving way, no acute injury. X-rays show moderate tricompartmental osteoarthritis with joint space narrowing. PT completed x8 weeks, NSAIDs and Tylenol provide partial relief. Weight 198 lbs, BMI 32. ASSESSMENT: Moderate bilateral knee osteoarthritis. PLAN: Requesting knee arthroscopy for pain relief.

PATIENT: 31-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: First-time generalized tonic-clonic seizure yesterday. HISTORY: Witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes, with post-ictal confusion lasting 20 minutes. No prior seizure history. No family history of epilepsy. No fever, no recent illness, no head trauma. No medications. Toxicology screen negative. Labs: sodium, glucose, CBC all normal. EEG performed showing focal slowing in left temporal region. PHYSICAL EXAM: Normal neurological exam post-ictally. ASSESSMENT: New onset seizure with focal EEG abnormality, requiring neuroimaging to exclude structural cause. PLAN: Urgent MRI brain with and without contrast.

chief_complaint: Chronic daily headache, normal prior workup
transcription: PATIENT: 28-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Daily headache for 2 years. HISTORY: Chronic daily headache, tension-type pattern, bilateral, non-pulsating, mild-moderate intensity. No nausea, no photophobia. No neurological symptoms. No red flag features. Prior CT brain 18 months ago: normal. Prior MRI brain without contrast 12 months ago: normal. Treated with amitriptyline and topiramate with partial response. ASSESSMENT: Chronic daily headache, tension-type, stable, prior imaging normal x2. PLAN: Requesting repeat MRI brain with contrast.

PATIENT: 71-year-old male. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Bilateral leg pain and weakness worsened with walking (neurogenic claudication). HISTORY: Progressive bilateral leg pain, heaviness, and tingling with ambulation greater than 1 block, relieved by sitting or leaning forward. Symptoms present 18 months, worsening for 6 months. Completed 10 weeks PT, acetaminophen and duloxetine for pain. MRI lumbar spine: severe L3-L4 and L4-L5 central canal stenosis with bilateral foraminal narrowing. PHYSICAL EXAM: Wide-based gait, positive bicycle test, decreased sensation bilateral lower extremities L4-L5 distribution. Strength 4/5 bilateral hip flexors. ASSESSMENT: Lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication, severe. Conservative management ongoing, requesting epidural steroid injection for symptomatic relief prior to surgical evaluation.

PATIENT: 58-year-old male. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Follow-up 3 months post NSTEMI. HISTORY: NSTEMI 3 months ago, treated with PCI and drug-eluting stent to LAD. Currently on aspirin, clopidogrel, atorvastatin, metoprolol, lisinopril. Symptoms: occasional mild exertional dyspnea with heavy exertion but no chest pain. Cardiac rehab completed 8 weeks. Echo post-MI: EF 45%, inferior wall hypokinesis. PHYSICAL EXAM: BP 122/76, HR 58. No signs of heart failure. ASSESSMENT: Stable CAD post-PCI, new exertional symptoms requiring functional assessment. PLAN: Requesting nuclear stress test to evaluate for ischemia in context of new symptoms.

PATIENT: 7-year-old male. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Loud snoring, restless sleep, behavioral issues at school. HISTORY: Parents report loud snoring nightly since age 4, witnessed apneas, enuresis, hyperactivity. Teacher reports attention difficulties in class. Tonsils grade 3+ bilaterally on prior exam. Adenoid hypertrophy on lateral neck X-ray. Growth: 25th percentile height, 20th percentile weight, crossing percentile lines downward. PHYSICAL EXAM: Mouth breathing, hyponasal speech, tonsillar enlargement. ASSESSMENT: Pediatric obstructive sleep apnea, likely secondary to adenotonsillar hypertrophy. PLAN: Polysomnography to confirm diagnosis and severity before surgical referral.

PATIENT: 22-year-old female college athlete (basketball). CHIEF COMPLAINT: Right knee instability after ACL tear 3 months ago. HISTORY: Complete ACL tear confirmed on MRI following non-contact pivoting injury during game. Patient reports significant functional instability — giving way episodes during activities of daily living. Unable to return to sport. No concurrent meniscal tear on MRI. Completed 12 weeks prehabilitation PT, quad strength 85% of contralateral side. ASSESSMENT: Complete ACL tear with functional instability, high-demand athletic patient desiring return to competitive sport. PLAN: ACL reconstruction via knee arthroscopy.

chief_complaint: Progressive cognitive decline, early dementia workup
transcription: PATIENT: 72-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Progressive memory loss over 18 months, family concerned. HISTORY: Insidious onset forgetfulness, word-finding difficulty, getting lost in familiar areas. MMSE score 22/30. Affects ADLs — missed medications, forgot appointments. No prior neuroimaging. Labs: TSH, B12, folate, RPR, CBC, metabolic panel all normal. No vascular risk factors. PHYSICAL EXAM: Alert, oriented x2, decreased recall and naming on exam. Gait normal. ASSESSMENT: Mild cognitive impairment, etiology to be determined. Structural brain imaging required to exclude reversible causes (normal pressure hydrocephalus, subdural hematoma, tumor) and characterize atrophy pattern. PLAN: MRI brain with and without contrast.

PATIENT: 66-year-old male scheduled for elective right total hip replacement. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Pre-operative cardiac clearance. HISTORY: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia. Functional capacity estimated at 4-5 METs (can walk on flat ground, climb one flight of stairs). No chest pain, no prior MI, no arrhythmia. Resting ECG: normal sinus rhythm with non-specific ST changes. Revised Cardiac Risk Index: 2 factors (diabetes on insulin, prior ischemic heart disease not confirmed). ASSESSMENT: Intermediate surgical risk, intermediate clinical risk. Functional capacity borderline, stress testing requested to guide perioperative management. PLAN: Exercise stress echo for pre-operative cardiac risk stratification.

PATIENT: 52-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: CPAP intolerance, requesting alternative therapy evaluation. HISTORY: Diagnosed with moderate OSA (AHI 22) via polysomnography 3 years ago. Prescribed CPAP 10 cmH2O. Compliance data: average 2.1 hours/night, 60% of nights below 4 hours. Complaints: claustrophobia, pressure intolerance, mask leak. Residual AHI on CPAP: 14. Daytime sleepiness persists (Epworth 13). Tried 3 different mask types. ASSESSMENT: CPAP-intolerant OSA, residual disease. Requesting repeat PSG for CPAP pressure adjustment and consideration of BIPAP or ASV therapy. PLAN: Split-night polysomnography for re-titration.

PATIENT: 49-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Recurrent right leg pain 18 months after L5-S1 discectomy. HISTORY: L5-S1 microdiscectomy 18 months ago for right S1 radiculopathy with good initial recovery. Over past 4 months, recurrence of right leg pain in S1 distribution, rated 5-6/10. No new weakness or bowel/bladder symptoms. MRI lumbar spine post-op: epidural fibrosis at L5-S1 with possible recurrent disc herniation vs. scar tissue, right S1 nerve root compression. PT trial x6 weeks: minimal improvement. ASSESSMENT: Failed back surgery syndrome with recurrent right S1 radiculopathy. PLAN: Fluoroscopic right S1 transforaminal epidural steroid injection for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes.

PATIENT: 26-year-old female. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Sudden painful loss of vision right eye over 3 days. HISTORY: Progressive blurring and pain with eye movement in right eye, developed over 3 days. No prior similar episodes. No other neurological symptoms at this time. Ophthalmology exam: reduced visual acuity right eye 20/100, reduced color vision, relative afferent pupillary defect, swollen optic disc. VEP: prolonged P100 latency right eye. ASSESSMENT: Clinical diagnosis of right optic neuritis. MRI brain with and without contrast urgently indicated to evaluate for demyelinating lesions consistent with multiple sclerosis and guide treatment decision for IV methylprednisolone.

PATIENT: 19-year-old male, competitive swimmer. CHIEF COMPLAINT: Palpitations and near-syncope during intense training for 2 months. HISTORY: Episodes of rapid irregular heartbeat during high-intensity training, one episode of near-syncope during 400m sprint at practice. No prior cardiac history. Family history: maternal uncle died suddenly at age 32 (cause unknown). Resting ECG: sinus bradycardia HR 48, early repolarization pattern, borderline voltage criteria for LVH. Echo: normal LV size and function, no LVOTO. ASSESSMENT: Exertional palpitations with near-syncope in competitive athlete, concerning for exercise-induced arrhythmia. Resting ECG changes require further evaluation. PLAN: Exercise stress test with continuous ECG monitoring to evaluate for arrhythmia provocation.
